HS

SUITABILITY – SKILLS AND TRAINING

Previously acted as a civilian participant ___________________ Complied with instruction from handlers

REASON THAT IT IS IMPRACTICABLE FOR A LAW ENFORCEMENT OFFICER:

Need to act immediately because HS is currently in communications with the ECN and there is no time to facilitate a LEP introduction ECN expect HS to follow instruction without delay and would be suspicious should a person unknown to them be introduced at this stage The HS needs to respond without delay and follow the instruction of the ECN – need to maintain HS credibility for safety reasons.

REASON WHY THE CIVILIAN PARTICIPANT REQUIRES CONDUCT TO PARTICIPATE:

To not engage would raise their suspicions and compromise the operation

sections.h:
Criminal Investigation (Covert Powers) Act 2012

URGENT APPLICATION PROCESS - A GUIDE FOR INVESTIGATORS

An urgent application is made verbally to the Authorising Officer. It can be made in person or over the telephone. However, whenever possible, the application should be made in person.

The Criminal Investigation (Covert Powers) Act 2012 (the Act) provides that an investigator can make an urgent application for an authority or a variation to an authority, if he or she has reason to believe that the delay caused by making a written application may affect the success of the operation. You must provide these reasons as part of any application made.

To commence the urgent application process, you must contact the Controlled Operations Supervisor of the Special Operations Application Unit (SOAU). They will determine if your application meets the required criteria of an urgent application.

If approved for progression, the Controlled Operations Supervisor will make the necessary arrangements with the Authorising Officer, the Commander of State Crime for availability. In the absence of the Commander of State Crime, the Authorising Officer will be sought as per the Delegation Schedule.

To prepare for an urgent application, refer to and complete the 3. Applicant’s Record in Writing (applicant notes).doc which is available on the SOAU Bluebook page.

The template can be used to ensure that your verbal application addresses the necessary legislative requirements. If you require assistance to complete the template, please contact the Controlled Operations Team.

When you are making your application to the Authorising Officer, you will require a ‘scribe’. The ‘scribe’ for the purposes of the Urgent Application process will be a staff member from the Controlled Operations Team, most likely the Controlled Operations Supervisor.

Under the Act, you are required, as soon as practicable after making an urgent application, to make a ‘record in writing’ of the application and give a copy of it, to the Authorising Officer. Controlled Operations will assist with this and be the conduit as required.

The template is a useful basis for the ‘record in writing’. The ‘record in writing’ is to be drafted by the Controlled Operations Team, and signed by the Applicant.

Once the ‘record in writing” has been completed, the Controlled Operations Supervisor is responsible for emailing the document directly to the Authorising Officer. An acknowledgement of receipt of the ‘record in writing’ is to be obtained from the Authorising Officer, pursuant to section 10(9) of the Act and included within the file.

Wherever possible, the ‘record in writing’ should be provided to the Authorising Officer within 48 hours, after the urgent application has been made. If you are unable to comply with this time period, the ‘record in writing’ must be provided as soon as practicable. Please detail the reasons for the delay in a covering email to the Authorising Officer.

You are required to submit a retrospective Tasking Request Form (TRF) within 24 hours of making an urgent application for an authority. A TRF is not required for a variation to an authority.

sections.h:
Urgent Application Process – Investigator Guidelines

Under the Criminal Investigation (Covert Powers) Act 2012 (WA)

1. Purpose of Urgent Applications

Urgent applications are permitted when delay in making a written application may jeopardise the success of a covert operation. This provision ensures operational agility while maintaining legal compliance.

2. Initial Contact and Assessment

- Contact the Controlled Operations Supervisor at the Special Operations Application Unit (SOAU).

- The Supervisor will assess whether the situation meets the criteria for urgency under the Act.

3. Application Method

- Applications may be made verbally, either in person or via telephone.

- In-person applications are preferred whenever feasible.

4. Preparation for Application

- Complete the 'Applicant’s Record in Writing' template (available on the SOAU Bluebook page).

- This template ensures all legislative requirements are addressed in your verbal application.

- Seek assistance from the Controlled Operations Team if needed.

5. Authorising Officer Coordination

- If the application is approved for progression:

- The Controlled Operations Supervisor will arrange contact with the Commander of State Crime (Authorising Officer).

- If unavailable, an alternative Authorising Officer will be contacted as per the Delegation Schedule.

6. Role of the Scribe

- A scribe is required during the verbal application.

- The scribe will be a member of the Controlled Operations Team, typically the Supervisor.

7. Post-Application Requirements

- As soon as practicable, a written record of the verbal application must be created.

- The Controlled Operations Team will draft this record using the template and submit it to the Authorising Officer.

- The Applicant must sign the written record.

8. Submission and Acknowledgement

- The Controlled Operations Supervisor will email the signed record to the Authorising Officer.

- An acknowledgement of receipt must be obtained and filed, as required under section 10(9) of the Act.

9. Timeframes

- The written record should be submitted within 48 hours of the verbal application.

- If this is not possible, submit it as soon as practicable, with a covering email explaining the delay.

10. Retrospective Tasking Request Form (TRF)

- Submit a TRF within 24 hours of making an urgent application for a new authority.

- TRFs are not required for variations to existing authorities.

11. Compliance and Oversight

- The Corruption and Crime Commission conducts annual inspections to ensure compliance with the Act.

- WA Police have demonstrated high levels of compliance, but all applications must be carefully documented to avoid technical breaches.

Annexure 1. Urgent Application for Authority – SOAU OIC Checklist Annexure 2. Urgent Application – Record in writing

Annexure 3. Section 10 Criminal Investigation (Covert Powers) Act 2012 (WA) Annexure 4. Section 15 Criminal Investigation (Covert Powers) Act 2012 (WA)

sections.t: 10.	Applications for authorities to conduct controlled operations
sections.h:
(1) A law enforcement officer of a law enforcement agency may apply to the authorising officer for the agency for authority to conduct a controlled operation on behalf of the agency.

(2) An application for an authority may be made —

(a) in writing (a formal application); or

(b) orally (an urgent application), if the applicant has reason to believe that the delay caused by making a formal application may affect the success of the operation.

(3) A formal application must be in a physical form, signed by the applicant.

(4) However if it is impracticable in the circumstances for a physical document to be delivered to the authorising officer, a formal application may take the form of —

(a) a fax; or

(b) an email or other electronic document, in which case the document need not be signed.

(5) An urgent application may be made in person, by telephone or any other electronic means.

(6) Nothing in this Division prevents an application for an authority being made in respect of a controlled operation that has been the subject of a previous application.

(7) In any application, whether formal or urgent, the applicant must —

(a) provide sufficient information to enable the authorising officer to decide whether or not to grant the application; and

(b) state whether or not the proposed operation, or any other controlled operation in respect of the same criminal activity, has been the subject of an earlier application for an authority or variation of an authority and, if so, whether or not the authority or variation was granted.

(8) The authorising officer may require the applicant to furnish such additional information concerning the proposed controlled operation as is necessary for the authorising officer’s proper consideration of the application.

(9) As soon as practicable after making an urgent application, the applicant must make a record in writing of the application and give a copy of it to the authorising officer.
sections.b: (1) A law enforcement officer of a law enforcement agency may apply to the authorising officer for the agency for authority to conduct a controlled operation on behalf of the agency. (2) An application for an authority may be made — (a) in writing (a formal application); or (b) orally (an urgent application), if the applicant has reason to believe that the delay caused by making a formal application may affect the success of the operation. (3) A formal application must be in a physical form, signed by the applicant. (4) However if it is impracticable in the circumstances for a physical document to be delivered to the authorising officer, a formal application may take the form of — (a) a fax; or (b) an email or other electronic document, in which case the document need not be signed. (5) An urgent application may be made in person, by telephone or any other electronic means. (6) Nothing in this Division prevents an application for an authority being made in respect of a controlled operation that has been the subject of a previous application. (7) In any application, whether formal or urgent, the applicant must — (a) provide sufficient information to enable the authorising officer to decide whether or not to grant the application; and (b) state whether or not the proposed operation, or any other controlled operation in respect of the same criminal activity, has been the subject of an earlier application for an authority or variation of an authority and, if so, whether or not the authority or variation was granted. (8) The authorising officer may require the applicant to furnish such additional information concerning the proposed controlled operation as is necessary for the authorising officer’s proper consideration of the application. (9) As soon as practicable after making an urgent application, the applicant must make a record in writing of the application and give a copy of it to the authorising officer.

sections.h:
(1) An authority to conduct a controlled operation may be granted —

(a) in writing (a formal authority); or

(b) orally (an urgent authority), if the authorising officer is satisfied that the delay caused by granting a formal authority may affect the success of the operation.

(2) A formal authority must be in a physical form, signed by the authorising officer.

(3) However if it is impracticable in the circumstances for a physical document to be delivered to the applicant, a formal authority may take the form of —

(a) a fax; or

(b) an email or other electronic document, in which case the document need not be signed.

(4) An urgent authority may be granted in person, by telephone or any other electronic means.

(5) Nothing in this Division prevents an authority being granted in respect of a controlled operation that has been the subject of a previous authority.

(6) An authority, whether formal or urgent, must do all of the following —

(a) state the name, and rank or position, of the person granting the authority;

(b) identify the principal law enforcement officer and, if the principal law enforcement officer is not the applicant for the authority, the name of the applicant;

(c) state whether the authority is for a cross-border controlled operation or a local controlled operation;

(d) for a cross-border controlled operation, state whether it is to be, or is likely to be, conducted —

(i) in this jurisdiction and in one or more participating jurisdictions specified in the authority; or

(ii) in one or more participating jurisdictions specified in the authority;

(e) state whether the authority is a formal authority or an urgent authority;

(f) identify each person who may engage in controlled conduct for the purposes of the controlled operation;

(g) identify the nature of the criminal activity (including the suspected relevant offences) in respect of which the controlled conduct is to be engaged in;

(h) identify —

(i) with respect to the law enforcement participants, the nature of the controlled conduct that those participants may engage in; and

(ii) with respect to the civilian participants, the particular controlled conduct, if any, that each such participant may engage in;

(i) identify (to the extent known) any suspect;

(j) specify the period of validity of the authority, being a period not exceeding 6 months in the case of a formal authority or 7 days in the case of an urgent authority;

(k) specify any conditions to which the conduct of the operation is subject;

(l) state the date and time when the authority is granted;

(m) identify (to the extent known) —

(i) the nature and quantity of any illicit goods that will be involved in the operation; and

(ii) the route through which those goods will pass in the course of the operation.

(7) A person is sufficiently identified for the purposes of subsection (6)(f) if the person is identified —

(a) by an assumed name under which the person is operating; or

(b) by a code name or code number,

as long as the assumed name, code name or code number can be matched to the person’s identity by reference to records kept by the chief officer.

(8) The authorising officer must, as soon as practicable after granting an urgent authority, make a record in writing of the particulars referred to in subsection (6) relating to the authority.
sections.b: (1) An authority to conduct a controlled operation may be granted — (a) in writing (a formal authority); or (b) orally (an urgent authority), if the authorising officer is satisfied that the delay caused by granting a formal authority may affect the success of the operation. (2) A formal authority must be in a physical form, signed by the authorising officer. (3) However if it is impracticable in the circumstances for a physical document to be delivered to the applicant, a formal authority may take the form of — (a) a fax; or (b) an email or other electronic document, in which case the document need not be signed. (4) An urgent authority may be granted in person, by telephone or any other electronic means. (5) Nothing in this Division prevents an authority being granted in respect of a controlled operation that has been the subject of a previous authority. (6) An authority, whether formal or urgent, must do all of the following — (a) state the name, and rank or position, of the person granting the authority; (b) identify the principal law enforcement officer and, if the principal law enforcement officer is not the applicant for the authority, the name of the applicant; (c) state whether the authority is for a cross-border controlled operation or a local controlled operation; (d) for a cross-border controlled operation, state whether it is to be, or is likely to be, conducted — (i) in this jurisdiction and in one or more participating jurisdictions specified in the authority; or (ii) in one or more participating jurisdictions specified in the authority; (e) state whether the authority is a formal authority or an urgent authority; (f) identify each person who may engage in controlled conduct for the purposes of the controlled operation; (g) identify the nature of the criminal activity (including the suspected relevant offences) in respect of which the controlled conduct is to be engaged in; (h) identify — (i) with respect to the law enforcement participants, the nature of the controlled conduct that those participants may engage in; and (ii) with respect to the civilian participants, the particular controlled conduct, if any, that each such participant may engage in; (i) identify (to the extent known) any suspect; (j) specify the period of validity of the authority, being a period not exceeding 6 months in the case of a formal authority or 7 days in the case of an urgent authority; (k) specify any conditions to which the conduct of the operation is subject; (l) state the date and time when the authority is granted; (m) identify (to the extent known) — (i) the nature and quantity of any illicit goods that will be involved in the operation; and (ii) the route through which those goods will pass in the course of the operation. (7) A person is sufficiently identified for the purposes of subsection (6)(f) if the person is identified — (a) by an assumed name under which the person is operating; or (b) by a code name or code number, as long as the assumed name, code name or code number can be matched to the person’s identity by reference to records kept by the chief officer. (8) The authorising officer must, as soon as practicable after granting an urgent authority, make a record in writing of the particulars referred to in subsection (6) relating to the authority.
